original_theorem: Prove that for any positive real numbers $a_1, a_2, \\ldots, a_n$, the following inequality holds:\n$$ \\sum_{k=1}^{n} k a_{k} \\leq \\binom{n}{2} + \\sum_{k=1}^{n} a_{k}^{k} $$
original_proof: For each positive integer $k$ and positive real number $a_k$, we apply the arithmetic mean‚Äďgeometric mean inequality to the numbers $a_k^k$ and $k-1$ copies of 1. This gives $$a_k^k + (k-1) \ge k a_k$$. This inequality holds for every $k = 1, 2, \dots, n$. Summing these inequalities over all $k$, we obtain $$\sum_{k=1}^{n}  ig(a_k^k + (k-1) ig) \ge \sum_{k=1}^{n} k a_k$$.The sum of the integers $0, 1, \dots, n-1$ is $ inom{n}{2}$, so we can write $$\sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k^k +  inom{n}{2} \ge \sum_{k=1}^{n} k a_k$$. Rewriting this inequality gives the desired result: $$\sum_{k=1}^{n} k a_k \le  inom{n}{2} + \sum_{k=1}^{n} a_k^k$$.
